title: How and why to oil your hair
excerpt: Ayurveda has long prescribed a simple daily ritual called Shiroabhyanga, or head oiling, not just for healthy hair but for calmer nerves and deeper sleep. Here is what the classical texts say and how to make it work in your week.
at_a_glance: Shiroabhyanga, or head oiling, is a classical Ayurvedic dinacharya practice for hair, scalp, and nervous-system health.
Sesame oil is the classical all-season base; coconut oil suits summer and Pitta types; Bhringraj oil is the most cited growth-support preparation.
Apply warm oil to the scalp (not just the hair), massage for five to ten minutes, and leave for at least thirty minutes before washing off.

body.heading: the everyday ritual you probably skipped
paragraphs: Most of us grew up watching an elder in the family sit in a patch of Sunday morning sunlight, someone quietly massaging warm oil into their scalp. It felt unhurried, almost ceremonial. Then life got busy, dry shampoo arrived, and the oil went to the back of the shelf. If your hair has felt brittle, your scalp itchy, or your mind wired at bedtime, Ayurveda would say those things are connected, and that the remedy is exactly the ritual you dropped.

The practice is called Shiroabhyanga, which means literally 'oil massage of the head' (shira = head, abhyanga = oil application). The classical texts place it inside dinacharya, the recommended daily or weekly routine for maintaining health. Ashtanga Hridaya describes daily head-oiling among the core habits that keep the sense organs sharp, the hair lustrous, and the sleep sound (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Caraka Samhita agrees, listing Shiroabhyanga among the practices that prevent premature greying, hair loss, and dryness of the scalp while also calming the mind (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

body.heading: what the granthas say about hair and the head
paragraphs: In Ayurvedic anatomy, the head is considered one of the most vital marma regions, a site where life energy concentrates. The scalp is crossed by channels that connect to the eyes, ears, and nervous system. Dryness here, caused by excess Vata dosha, is therefore not a cosmetic issue alone. It is seen as a sign of depleted nourishment reaching the whole upper body.

Bhavaprakasha notes that the hair itself (referred to in the context of kesha) draws its nourishment from the bone tissue (asthi dhatu), because hair is considered a by-product of that tissue layer (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda). When bone tissue is well nourished, the hair reflects it. When it is depleted, hair thins and falls. Regular oil application is one of the practical ways the texts recommend supporting this nourishment from the outside in, allowing the oil to penetrate the scalp, reduce local dryness, and keep the channels supple.

Caraka Samhita specifically mentions that Shiroabhyanga prevents the scalp from cracking, reduces headache caused by Vata aggravation, and supports the sense organs in the head (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The connection to sleep is direct: Vata governs the nervous system, and calming it through oil and gentle massage at the scalp is one of the classical prescriptions for restlessness at night.

body.heading: how to actually do it
paragraphs: The classical method is straightforward. Warm a small amount of oil, perhaps two to three tablespoons, by placing the bottle in a cup of hot water for a few minutes. Part your hair into sections and apply the oil directly to the scalp with your fingertips, not just to the hair shaft. Then massage gently in slow, circular motions across the whole scalp for five to ten minutes. The pressure should feel pleasant, not vigorous.

Leave the oil on for at least thirty minutes. Longer is fine, including an overnight application. Wash off with a mild shampoo, using warm rather than hot water. Hot water strips the scalp further and aggravates Vata. Doing this two or three times a week is the classical recommendation; daily oiling was prescribed for those with strong Vata constitution or significant dryness.

On oil choice, Ashtanga Hridaya favours sesame oil as the base for most constitutions because it is warming and penetrating, ideal for balancing Vata (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Coconut oil is cooling and is better suited for summers or for Pitta-dominant constitutions with scalp heat or inflammation. Bhringraj oil, prepared with Eclipta alba as the primary herb in a sesame base, is among the most cited preparations in later texts including Bhavaprakasha for promoting hair growth and reducing greying. Plain sesame oil, if you have nothing else, is a perfectly sound starting point.

body.heading: when to see a doctor or vaidya
paragraphs: Shiroabhyanga is a gentle, low-risk practice for most people. However, please consult a doctor or qualified Ayurvedic vaidya if you notice sudden or significant hair loss that is not explained by seasonal change, patchy bald spots, persistent scalp inflammation, redness or oozing sores on the scalp, or hair loss accompanied by fatigue, unexplained weight change, or other systemic symptoms. These can point to medical conditions such as alopecia areata, thyroid disorders, nutritional deficiencies, or scalp infections that need proper diagnosis and treatment.

This article is general information drawn from classical Ayurvedic literature. It is not a diagnosis and is not a substitute for personalised medical care. If you are pregnant, have a known skin condition, or are on medication, check with your doctor before beginning any oil-based scalp treatment.

try_this_today.title: Sunday warm-oil ritual
try_this_today.description: Warm two tablespoons of sesame or coconut oil in a cup of hot water. Part hair into four sections and apply oil directly to the scalp. Massage in slow circles for eight to ten minutes, then wrap in a warm towel and rest for thirty minutes before washing off.

try_this_today.title: Pre-bed scalp press
try_this_today.description: On nights you feel wired or restless, apply a small amount of warm sesame oil to the crown and the soles of your feet. This is the abbreviated dinacharya Vata-calming sequence and many people find it shortens the time to sleep.

try_this_today.title: Switch oil by season
try_this_today.description: Use sesame oil through the cooler months when Vata is naturally elevated. Switch to coconut oil in peak summer when the scalp tends to feel hot or itchy. Both are sound classical choices.

try_this_today.title: Avoid these habits alongside oiling
try_this_today.description: Very hot water, tight ponytails immediately after oiling, and vigorous towel-rubbing all counteract the benefit. Pat the scalp dry gently and keep the hair loose for a few hours after washing.

words_to_know.definition: Therapeutic oil massage of the head and scalp; shira means head, abhyanga means oil application.
Daily routine prescribed in Ayurveda for maintaining health and balance across the seasons.
One of the three doshas, governing movement, dryness, and the nervous system; excess Vata is the primary cause of hair and scalp dryness.
Bone tissue, one of the seven bodily tissues; hair is considered a by-product of asthi and reflects its health.
Hair, as referred to in Ayurvedic texts; its quality is seen as a direct indicator of internal tissue nourishment.
Vital energy points on the body; the head region contains several important marmas connected to the sense organs.

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana · Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Caraka Samhita, Sharirasthana
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
